BriefingTemplate: High-value asset pinned down in {location}. Three factions want them dead. Get them out — or don't come back.
SuccessText: {leader} pulls the asset out clean. No traces.
PartialText: Asset secured, but the exit was loud. Someone saw faces.
FailureText: The extraction collapsed. {leader}'s team barely got out.
CatastropheText: Total failure. The asset is gone and so is any deniability.

Nodes.Text: {leader} reviews the dossier. The asset is holed up in a condemned high-rise on the east side of {location}. Two known exit routes — the service tunnel and the rooftop. Intel says {target} has a patrol rotation with a gap at 0300.

Choices.Text: Go through the service tunnel. Quiet, controlled.
Choices.Aftermath: The tunnel it is. {leader} signals the team to move low and slow.

Choices.Text: Rooftop extraction. Fast, visible, overwhelming.
Choices.Aftermath: Loud and fast. {leader} preps the breach charges.

Nodes.Text: Halfway to the asset, {leader}'s earpiece crackles. A third party — unidentified — is converging on the building. They're not {target}'s people. The team has maybe four minutes before the situation gets very crowded.

Choices.Text: Accelerate. Beat them to the asset.
Choices.Aftermath: No time for finesse. The team doubles their pace.

Choices.Text: Set an ambush. Let the third party walk into us.
Choices.Aftermath: Cold calculus. {leader} positions the team in the stairwell.

Choices.Text: Divert them. False signal, wrong floor.
Choices.Aftermath: A ghost ping on their comms. They'll chase shadows for ten minutes.

Nodes.Text: The asset is in sight. But they're not alone — a handler, armed, watching the door. The asset's eyes say 'help me.' The handler's hand is on a detonator.

Choices.Text: Talk them down. Promise safe passage.
Choices.Aftermath: Words as weapons. {leader} steps forward, hands visible.

Choices.Text: Headshot. Clean. Before the thumb moves.
Choices.Aftermath: One shot. The handler drops. The asset screams.

Choices.Text: Kill the lights. Move in the dark.
Choices.Aftermath: Darkness. Then silence. Then movement.

BriefingTemplate: Board wants {target}'s leadership removed. Permanently. Make it look like anything except what it is.
SuccessText: The target is neutralised. No fingerprints. No questions.
PartialText: Target down, but the scene is messy. Someone will dig.
FailureText: The target survived. They'll know someone tried.
CatastropheText: Botched. Target alive, team compromised, bodies left behind.

Nodes.Text: {leader} studies the target's routine. Three windows: a morning jog through the industrial district, a weekly dinner at a private club, and a Saturday visit to a mistress in the warehouse quarter. Each has trade-offs.

Choices.Text: The jog. Open space, low witnesses, hard to stage.
Choices.Aftermath: Dawn operation. {leader} marks the route on the map.

Choices.Text: The dinner. Poison the meal. Classic.
Choices.Aftermath: Elegant. If the kitchen staff cooperate.

Choices.Text: The mistress. Compromise the location, stage an accident.
Choices.Aftermath: Messy in a different way. {leader} starts building the cover story.

Nodes.Text: The target has a child. Seven years old. They'll be present during the window. There is no clean version of this that doesn't risk the child seeing everything.

Choices.Text: Proceed as planned. Collateral is the client's problem.
Choices.Aftermath: The team goes quiet. {leader} doesn't look at anyone.

Choices.Text: Create a diversion. Get the child away first.
Choices.Aftermath: More moving parts. More risk. But the team breathes easier.

Choices.Text: Abort. Find another window without the child.
Choices.Aftermath: Delay costs. The board won't be pleased. But some lines hold.

Nodes.Text: Final approach. {leader} has eyes on the target. One signal and it's done. The team is in position. The only sound is breathing.

Choices.Text: Execute.
Choices.Aftermath: It's done.

Choices.Text: Make it look natural. Take the extra time.
Choices.Aftermath: Patience. The hardest weapon to wield.

BriefingTemplate: A data vault buried under {location}'s industrial zone. Air-gapped. Armed guards. The payload is worth more than your entire division's budget.
SuccessText: Data secured. {leader} delivers the payload personally.
PartialText: Partial dump. Some files were encrypted beyond reach.
FailureText: Locked out. The intrusion was detected mid-transfer.
CatastropheText: Counter-intrusion triggered. The team's identities may be burned.

Nodes.Text: The facility blueprints are old but legible. {leader} identifies two approaches: a physical intrusion through the ventilation system, or a social engineering play — posing as a maintenance contractor.

Choices.Text: Ventilation. No human contact, no variables.
Choices.Aftermath: Tight spaces. No margin for error.

Choices.Text: Maintenance cover. Walk in the front door.
Choices.Aftermath: Confidence is the best skeleton key.

Nodes.Text: Inside the server room. The air-gap is real — no wireless, no network. The data has to be physically copied. But there's a guard rotation in six minutes and the transfer will take eight.

Choices.Text: Overwrite the guard logs. Buy time.
Choices.Aftermath: Digital sleight of hand. The cameras see an empty room.

Choices.Text: Neutralise the guards quietly. Zip ties, dark corners.
Choices.Aftermath: Non-lethal. Probably. {leader} doesn't ask.

Choices.Text: Grab what you can in six minutes. Partial is better than caught.
Choices.Aftermath: Half the payload. All of the team.

Nodes.Text: Transfer complete. But the exit is compromised — a security team is sweeping the corridor. {leader} has the data drive.

Choices.Text: Bluff through. Flash the maintenance badge.
Choices.Aftermath: Smile. Nod. Walk.

Choices.Text: Emergency exit. Blow the fire suppression, run.
Choices.Aftermath: Halon gas and chaos. Good enough.

BriefingTemplate: {target}'s supply chain runs through {location}. Cripple it and they lose a quarter's revenue. The board wants economic damage, not bodies.
SuccessText: Infrastructure down. {target} will feel this for months.
PartialText: Partial disruption. They'll recover faster than hoped.
FailureText: The charges didn't detonate. Or the code didn't compile. Either way, nothing happened.
CatastropheText: Backfire. The sabotage triggered a cascade that hit civilian systems.

Nodes.Text: {leader} maps the logistics hub. Two critical nodes: the routing servers that coordinate shipments, and the physical rail junction that feeds the warehouse district. Hitting one hurts. Hitting both cripples.

Choices.Text: Cyber attack on the routing servers. Remote, surgical.
Choices.Aftermath: Digital warfare. Clean if it works.

Choices.Text: Physical sabotage on the rail junction. Old school.
Choices.Aftermath: Thermite and timing. {leader} likes the simplicity.

Choices.Text: Both. Simultaneous strike.
Choices.Aftermath: Maximum damage. Maximum exposure.

Nodes.Text: Mid-operation. A night-shift worker stumbles into the restricted area. Civilian. Scared. Reaching for a phone.

Choices.Text: Restrain and sedate. They wake up with a headache and a story.
Choices.Aftermath: Zip ties and chloroform. Not elegant, but no one dies.

Choices.Text: Silence them. Permanently. No witnesses.
Choices.Aftermath: A line crossed. The team knows it.

Choices.Text: Convince them they saw nothing. Flash a badge, tell a story.
Choices.Aftermath: A scared nod. They'll go home and try to forget.

Nodes.Text: Charges set. Timer running. But {leader} notices the blast radius will clip a residential water main. Hundreds of families lose water for weeks. The mission parameters didn't mention this.

Choices.Text: Detonate as planned. The mission comes first.
Choices.Aftermath: Collateral damage. The board won't care. The team will.

Choices.Text: Reposition the charges. Smaller blast, less damage.
Choices.Aftermath: Reduced impact. But the infrastructure still goes down.

BriefingTemplate: Someone inside {corp} is feeding intel to {target}. Find the mole. Then decide what to do with them.
SuccessText: Mole identified and handled. The leak is sealed.
PartialText: Mole found, but they had time to burn files first.
FailureText: Investigation stalled. The mole is still active.
CatastropheText: Wrong person accused. The real mole digs deeper.

Nodes.Text: {leader} has three suspects. A finance analyst with gambling debts, a logistics coordinator who recently started encrypting personal calls, and a senior handler whose field reports don't match satellite data.

Choices.Text: Digital surveillance. Monitor their devices, trace the leak.
Choices.Aftermath: Patience. Let the data tell the story.

Choices.Text: Interrogate all three. Pressure reveals truth.
Choices.Aftermath: Three rooms. Three conversations. One answer.

Choices.Text: Feed each suspect different false intel. See which version leaks.
Choices.Aftermath: The canary trap. Classic for a reason.

Nodes.Text: The mole is identified: it's the senior handler. Sixteen years of service. A family. And a reason — their child needs medical treatment that {corp}'s insurance won't cover. {target} offered to pay.

Choices.Text: Turn them. Double agent. Feed {target} disinformation.
Choices.Aftermath: Redemption through utility. The handler nods slowly.

Choices.Text: Disappear them. The corp can't afford the optics.
Choices.Aftermath: A family will wonder. {leader} files a transfer report that never happened.

Choices.Text: Expose them publicly. Let the system handle it.
Choices.Aftermath: By the book. The handler is escorted out in front of everyone.

Nodes.Text: Final step: the handler's dead drop. A physical location where they leave files for {target}'s courier. {leader} can seed it with poison data — or just shut it down.

Choices.Text: Poison the drop. Long game. Feed them what we want them to believe.
Choices.Aftermath: Misinformation is the gift that keeps giving.

Choices.Text: Burn it. Clean break. No loose ends.
Choices.Aftermath: Fire solves many problems.

BriefingTemplate: Long-range surveillance of {target}'s leadership. Plant an operative inside their operations for three weeks. The intel could reshape the board's strategy — or get someone killed.
SuccessText: Three weeks of intel. {target}'s playbook is wide open.
PartialText: Cover held but the operative was kept at arm's length. Surface intel only.
FailureText: Cover blown after ten days. Operative extracted under fire.
CatastropheText: Operative captured. {target} now knows what {corp} was looking for.

Nodes.Text: The cover identity needs depth. {leader} has two legends prepared: a disgruntled tech contractor looking for better pay, or a corporate headhunter scouting {target}'s talent pool.

Choices.Text: The contractor. Technical access, lower profile.
Choices.Aftermath: Boring is safe. The best covers always are.

Choices.Text: The headhunter. Social access, higher risk.
Choices.Aftermath: Charm offensive. Everyone likes someone who might offer them a raise.

Nodes.Text: Week two. The operative has gained trust but {target}'s security chief is suspicious. A loyalty test is coming — the operative will be asked to provide intel on {corp}. Real intel.

Choices.Text: Feed them stale data. True enough to pass, old enough to be harmless.
Choices.Aftermath: A tightrope. The data has to smell fresh.

Choices.Text: Give them real low-value intel. Burn a minor asset to protect the cover.
Choices.Aftermath: Sacrifice a pawn. The operative sinks deeper.

Choices.Text: Pull the operative out. The cover is too thin.
Choices.Aftermath: Early extraction. Less intel, but the operative lives.

Nodes.Text: Final day. The operative has the intel — but their handler at {target} has invited them to a private meeting. Could be a promotion inside the cover story. Could be a trap.

Choices.Text: Attend. Maximum intel, maximum risk.
Choices.Aftermath: One last roll of the dice.

Choices.Text: Extract now. Take what we have and run.
Choices.Aftermath: Clean exit. Good enough is good enough.
